critic_raw:
Section 9 states 'The temporal split automatically accounts for temporal trends' — but Section 3 describes a stratified random sampling split, not a temporal split. This is a direct internal contradiction. The claimed temporal protection is illusory; the design uses random splitting while asserting temporal guarantees that don't exist.

All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training set (correct), but the split itself is random — sensor windows from the same machine near a failure event will appear in both training and test sets. For a 7-day prediction window at 1-minute sampling, a machine failure generates approximately 10,080 correlated positive-labeled observations per machine per failure event. Random splitting distributes these temporally autocorrelated windows across train and test, creating near-duplicate test examples for patterns already seen in training.

The RNN receives multi-modal inputs (sensor sequences + encoded operator logs) while the logistic regression baseline receives only aggregated sensor statistics, explicitly without operator logs or context features. The design states 'the baseline does not receive operator logs or context features' as a deliberate design choice. This means any RNN advantage is confounded between: (a) RNN architecture vs. LR, (b) sequential modeling vs. aggregate statistics, and (c) operator log access vs. no operator logs. The hypothesis tests whether multi-modal integration improves prediction; the baseline lacks the second modality, so the experiment cannot test this claim at all — it tests modality access plus architecture simultaneously.

The F1 score primary metric is computed with an enforced minimum recall threshold of 0.85. This threshold is determined by a 'probability decision threshold' sweep to find the operating point satisfying recall ≥ 0.85. The F1 at this constrained threshold is a valid metric, but comparing two models at different thresholds (the optimal threshold for recall ≥ 0.85 will differ between RNN and LR) means they are not evaluated at comparable operating points.

Concatenating sensor sequence and operator log sequence into a single multimodal input sequence to a single LSTM conflates temporal alignment across modalities. Sensor readings occur at 1-minute intervals while operator logs occur infrequently and aperiodically — aligning them as co-temporal sequences in a single LSTM requires implicit assumptions about how to pad/align sparse log events with dense sensor readings that the design does not specify.
all_issues_raised: Section 9 claims temporal protection from 'the temporal split' but Section 3 describes stratified random sampling — there is no temporal split, making the claimed temporal protection an internal contradiction.

Random splitting of 36-month sensor time-series data with a 7-day prediction window distributes temporally autocorrelated positive-labeled windows from the same machine failure event across training and test sets — each failure generates ~10,080 correlated labeled windows that become near-duplicates across the split.

The RNN receives both sensor sequences and operator logs while the LR baseline receives only sensor statistics without logs — any observed advantage is confounded between model architecture, sequential representation, and modality access, making the multi-modal hypothesis untestable.

F1 is computed at a threshold that enforces recall ≥ 0.85, but this threshold will differ between the RNN and LR — comparing F1 scores computed at different operating points is not a valid model comparison.

Sensor readings (1-minute intervals) and operator logs (sparse, aperiodic) are concatenated as a single aligned temporal sequence for the LSTM — the temporal alignment procedure for sparse logs with dense sensors is unspecified, and this design choice fundamentally affects what cross-modal dependencies the LSTM can learn.
all_issues_adjudicated: Section 9 claims temporal protection from 'the temporal split' but Section 3 describes stratified random sampling — there is no temporal split, making the claimed temporal protection an internal contradiction.

Random splitting of 36-month sensor time-series data with a 7-day prediction window distributes temporally autocorrelated positive-labeled windows from the same machine failure event across training and test sets — each failure generates ~10,080 correlated labeled windows that become near-duplicates across the split.

The RNN receives both sensor sequences and operator logs while the LR baseline receives only sensor statistics without logs — any observed advantage is confounded between model architecture, sequential representation, and modality access, making the multi-modal hypothesis untestable.

F1 is computed at a threshold that enforces recall ≥ 0.85, but this threshold will differ between the RNN and LR — comparing F1 scores computed at different operating points is not a valid model comparison.

Sensor readings (1-minute intervals) and operator logs (sparse, aperiodic) are concatenated as a single aligned temporal sequence for the LSTM — the temporal alignment procedure for sparse logs with dense sensors is unspecified, and this design choice fundamentally affects what cross-modal dependencies the LSTM can learn.
